text.title: spatial-computing-overview Core Rules
text.summary: Migrated testable rules for spatial-computing-overview. Verify and refine before relying on for production.

rule.statement: Use precise XR terminology in all design documentation: AR (digital overlay on real world, passthrough camera or optical), VR (fully immersive synthetic environment), MR (real and digital objects interact in real time), XR (umbrella term for all three).
rule.rationale: Conflating AR and VR leads to wrong platform selection, incorrect interaction model assumptions, and deliverables that cannot be implemented on the target device.

rule.statement: For Apple Vision Pro: design eye-gaze + pinch as the primary interaction; no physical controller grip states; use visionOS window and volume primitives; assume high rendering quality and precision eye tracking.
rule.rationale: visionOS was designed around eye+hand input from the ground up; controller-centric patterns from other platforms do not translate and will feel unnatural.

rule.statement: For Meta Quest: design for both hand tracking and controller input as parallel paths; assume color passthrough for MR use cases; test comfort for the larger consumer user base including non-technical users.
rule.rationale: Quest's broad consumer install base includes users unfamiliar with 3D interfaces; simpler interaction models and robust onboarding are required.

rule.statement: For HoloLens and Magic Leap: design for factory and field use — glove compatibility, outdoor brightness, short interaction sequences between physical work tasks, voice as primary input when hands are occupied.
rule.rationale: Industrial XR users are performing physical tasks concurrently; interaction must be minimal and glove-compatible; content must be visible in high-ambient-light environments.
